# The Research Toolchain

You do not write research at a bare terminal. You write it in RStudio or
VS Code, in Jupyter notebooks, in Quarto documents, in Overleaf. Git
sits underneath all of these, and this chapter is about the seams: how
the editor's Git buttons map to the commands you already know, why
notebooks need special handling before Git can diff them sanely, and how
Quarto and Overleaf projects fit a version-controlled workflow.

A note on what is captured here. The notebook-diffing tools (`nbdime`,
`jupytext`) are run for real in the sandbox, so that output is genuine.
The IDE and Overleaf material is documented from current official
sources (verified 2026-06-22, logged in
`verification/2026-06-22-ch9-14-github-sources.md`) because those do not
run in this book's build environment. The `renv` explanation is
documented from the same sources and backed by a live path-A console
capture from a throwaway project on the author's Mac.

## The editor's Git pane is the same Git {.tier-beginner}

RStudio and VS Code both ship a graphical Git interface, and both are
thin convenience layers over the exact commands from Chapters 5, 7, and
9. RStudio's **Git pane** (enabled per project when the project folder is
a repo) stages files with a checkbox, opens a diff viewer, commits with
a message box, and has push/pull buttons and a history view. VS Code's
**Source Control** view does the same (stage, commit, branch, sync), and
its GitHub Pull Requests extension brings PR review (Chapter 11) into the
editor. Nothing about these changes what Git does: a click on "Commit" is
`git commit`, "Push" is `git push`, the diff is `git diff`. Learn the
commands first (you have) and the GUI is just faster for the daily loop.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The risk with a Git GUI is that it hides what it is doing until the
moment it cannot cope. A button labelled "Sync" may pull-then-push, or
merge, or rebase, depending on settings, and when it hits a conflict or a
rejected push it drops you back to exactly the situation Chapters 7 and
12 covered, now with less visible context. Treat the GUI as a fast path
for `status`/`add`/`commit`/`push`, and the moment anything looks
unusual, switch to the terminal where you can see the whole state.
:::

## Notebooks: why Git diffs them badly {.tier-beginner}

A Jupyter notebook (`.ipynb`) looks like cells of code and output, but
on disk it is a single JSON file. That JSON stores your source, an
`execution_count` for each cell, and the full cell outputs. So a plain
`git diff` after merely *re-running* a notebook, with no real code
change, is cluttered with execution counts and output churn:

```bash
$ git diff
diff --git a/analysis.ipynb b/analysis.ipynb
index 7696b37..c5c42f9 100644
--- a/analysis.ipynb
+++ b/analysis.ipynb
@@ -2,13 +2,13 @@
  "cells": [
   {
    "cell_type": "code",
-   "execution_count": 1,
+   "execution_count": 7,
    "id": "a1",
    "metadata": {},
    "outputs": [
-    {"name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": ["effect = -0.0189\n"]}
+    {"name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": ["effect = -0.0191\n"]}
    ],
-   "source": ["effect = -0.0189\n", "print(f\"effect = {effect}\")"]
+   "source": ["effect = -0.0191\n", "print(f\"effect = {effect}\")"]
```

Even in this tiny example the real change (one number) is buried among
metadata changes. In a real notebook, cell outputs include base64-encoded
images and long tables, so a one-line code edit can produce a diff
thousands of lines long that no reviewer can read, and merge conflicts in
that JSON are nearly impossible to resolve by hand. Two tools fix this.

## Notebooks, fix one: diff them as notebooks {.tier-advanced}

`nbdime` understands notebook structure and diffs cell by cell, labelling
exactly what changed (source, outputs, execution count) instead of
diffing raw JSON. The same change as above, through `nbdiff`:

```bash
$ nbdiff --no-color HEAD analysis.ipynb
nbdiff analysis.ipynb (HEAD) analysis.ipynb
--- analysis.ipynb (HEAD)  (no timestamp)
+++ analysis.ipynb  2026-02-16 09:05:00.000000
## replaced /cells/0/execution_count:
-  1
+  7

## modified /cells/0/outputs/0/text:
@@ -1 +1 @@
-effect = -0.0189
+effect = -0.0191

## modified /cells/0/source:
@@ -1,2 +1,2 @@
-effect = -0.0189
+effect = -0.0191
 print(f"effect = {effect}")
```

Now each change is named and grouped: the execution-count bump, the
output change, and the one-line source edit are separate and legible.
`nbdime` can also wire itself into Git so that `git diff` and
`git merge` route notebooks through it, but this is opt-in: you run
`nbdime config-git --enable` once, which registers the drivers in your
Git config.

```bash
$ nbdime config-git --enable
$ git config --local --get-regexp 'jupyternotebook'
diff.jupyternotebook.command git-nbdiffdriver diff
merge.jupyternotebook.driver git-nbmergedriver merge %O %A %B %L %P
merge.jupyternotebook.name jupyter notebook merge driver
```

After that your normal `git diff` on a notebook uses the cell-aware diff
above, and a web view (`nbdiff-web`) renders rich diffs in the browser.

## Notebooks, fix two: pair with a text file {.tier-advanced}

`jupytext` takes a different angle: keep a plain-text twin of the
notebook that Git tracks cleanly. Converting the notebook to a
"percent"-format script strips the JSON and outputs entirely, leaving
just the code in readable cells:

```bash
$ jupytext --to py:percent analysis.ipynb
[jupytext] Reading analysis.ipynb in format ipynb
[jupytext] Writing analysis.py in format py:percent
$ cat analysis.py
# %%
effect = -0.0191
print(f"effect = {effect}")
```

(The file also carries a small commented header recording the jupytext
and kernel versions.) That `--to` is a one-off conversion. To keep the
two files in sync as a *pair*, you set the formats once, which records
the pairing in the notebook's metadata so Jupyter (with jupytext
installed) regenerates the `.py` every time you save:

```bash
$ jupytext --set-formats ipynb,py:percent paired.ipynb
[jupytext] Reading paired.ipynb in format ipynb
[jupytext] Updating notebook metadata with '{"jupytext": {"formats": "ipynb,py:percent"}}'
[jupytext] Unchanged paired.ipynb
[jupytext] Updating paired.py
```

From then on you edit in Jupyter as usual and commit the `.py`, which
diffs and merges like any script. The notebook stays for interactive
work; the text twin is what version control sees.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Pick one notebook discipline and apply it before committing: either pair
with `jupytext` and track the text twin, or strip outputs on commit with
`nbstripout` (a separate tool whose `nbstripout --install` registers a
Git filter that clears outputs as files are staged) so only inputs are
versioned. `nbdime` diffs and merges notebooks; it is `nbstripout` that
strips outputs, so reach for the right one. A notebook committed with
stale embedded outputs is an active
reproducibility hazard: the saved numbers and figures may no longer match
what the code produces, and a reader cannot tell. Version the inputs;
regenerate the outputs.
:::

## Quarto projects under Git {.tier-beginner}

Quarto (this book is a Quarto project) mixes prose and executable code,
and the Git rule follows Chapter 8's logic: version the source, ignore
the rebuildable outputs, with one important exception. Add the render
output directory to `.gitignore` (`/_book/` for a book, `/_site/` for a
website), and Quarto itself adds `/.quarto/` on render. But the
top-level **`_freeze/`** directory, which caches the results of your
executable code, *should* be committed. Committing `_freeze/` lets a
collaborator or a CI job (Chapter 11) reproduce the rendered document
without re-running every computation, and Quarto re-runs only the
documents you actually changed on the next render.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
A minimal `.gitignore` for a Quarto project:

```text
/_book/
/.quarto/
# commit _freeze/ : it caches computed results
```

Documented from Quarto's project guidance; this book's own repository is
configured this way.
:::

## R projects: renv is uv.lock for R {.tier-advanced}

Chapter 8 committed a `uv.lock` to pin the Python environment. R's
equivalent is **renv**, and the workflow is the direct analogue.
`renv::init()` gives the project a private library and writes an initial
`renv.lock`; `renv::snapshot()` records the exact installed versions into
`renv.lock`; a collaborator runs `renv::restore()` to reinstall precisely
those versions. You commit `renv.lock` together with `.Rprofile`,
`renv/settings.json`, and `renv/activate.R` (renv writes a `.gitignore`
that keeps the library itself out of Git). The lockfile is the thing that
makes an R result reproducible, exactly as in the Python case.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Captured on the author's Mac (2026-06-23).** In a throwaway project
containing `library(dplyr)`, `renv::init()` wrote `~/renv-demo/renv.lock`
and pinned `dplyr` plus its dependency tree (`cli`, `glue`, `rlang`,
`vctrs`, `tibble`, and the rest). The project was then cloned without
its private library, and `renv::restore()` rebuilt the library from the
lockfile.

```bash
$ git status --short
A  .Rprofile
A  .gitignore
A  analysis.R
A  renv.lock
A  renv/.gitignore
A  renv/activate.R
A  renv/settings.json
```

```bash
$ Rscript -e 'renv::restore()'
# Bootstrapping renv 1.2.3 ---------------------------------------------------
- Downloading renv ... OK
- Installing renv  ... OK

- None of the packages recorded in the lockfile are currently installed.
- Use `renv::restore()` to restore the project library.
The following package(s) will be updated:
...
# Installing packages --------------------------------------------------------
✔ tidyselect 1.2.1                         [linked from cache]
✔ magrittr 2.0.4                           [linked from cache]
✔ R6 2.6.1                                 [linked from cache]
...
Successfully installed 15 packages in 9.5 milliseconds.
```

Full captures: `transcripts/14_renv_init.txt`,
`transcripts/14_renv_lock_head.txt`, `transcripts/14_renv_files.txt`,
`transcripts/14_renv_restore.txt`, and `transcripts/14_renv_status.txt`.
:::

## Overleaf and GitHub {.tier-advanced}

Many social-science papers are written in Overleaf while the code lives
on GitHub, and Overleaf can link the two. The connection is a **premium**
Overleaf feature: you link your Overleaf account to GitHub in Account
Settings, then create the Overleaf project with New Project -> Import from
GitHub, after which a menu action pushes and pulls between the Overleaf
project and the linked repository. The sync is explicit, not continuous:
you press the button to move changes each way.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Overleaf's sync has documented edges worth knowing before you rely on it.
You cannot link an *existing* Overleaf project to an *existing* GitHub
repo: the import creates the project from the repo, so set the direction
up front. Because the sync is manual and bidirectional, editing the same
file in both places between syncs produces a conflict you resolve by
hand, so treat one side (usually the GitHub repo) as the source of truth
and sync often. Overleaf also recommends keeping commits under ~100 files
and the project under ~100 MB. Documented from Overleaf's docs; not
exercised in this book's build.
:::

## What you can now do

You can connect Git to the tools you actually write in: drive the daily
loop from the RStudio or VS Code Git pane while knowing the commands
underneath, tame Jupyter notebooks so Git can diff and merge them (pair
with `jupytext` or diff with `nbdime`, and never commit stale outputs),
configure a Quarto project to ignore its outputs while committing the
`_freeze/` cache, pin an R environment with `renv` the way Chapter 8
pinned Python, and sync an Overleaf manuscript to GitHub with its edges
in mind. Chapter 15 gathers the whole project (code, pinned environment,
tagged release) into a formal replication package.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

1. In RStudio or VS Code, open your clone of the running example, make a
   one-line edit, and commit it from the GUI Git pane. Then run
   `git log -1` in a terminal and confirm the GUI made an ordinary
   commit.
2. Create a small notebook, commit it, re-run it without changing code,
   and run `git diff`. Identify which changes are noise (execution count,
   outputs) versus real source changes.
3. Install `jupytext`, pair the notebook with a `.py:percent` twin, and
   confirm the `.py` diffs cleanly when you change one line of code.
4. Set up a `.gitignore` for a Quarto project that ignores `/_book/` and
   `/.quarto/` but commits `_freeze/`. Explain in one sentence why
   `_freeze/` is the exception.
5. If you use R: run `renv::init()` in a project, `renv::snapshot()`, and
   inspect `renv.lock`. Name the files you would commit so a collaborator
   can `renv::restore()` your environment.
